context_relevancy.query: What type of airflow is required to maintain ultra-clean environments in aerospace operations?
context_chunks:
ronmental controls of a clean room, but which should be segregated from excessive dirt generation operation
Laminar Flow An airflow pattern in which the en- tire body of air within a confined area moves with uniform velocity along parallel lines
Laminar Flow Clean Room A clean room in which the laminar airflow characteristics predominate throughout the entire room, with a minimum of ed- dies
Laminar Flow Clean Work Station A clean work station in which the laminar airflow characteristics predominate throughout the entire airspace, with a minimum of eddies
CHAPTER 6 LAMINAR FLOW CLEAN ROOMS CROSS FLOW (CLASS 10,000) AND DOWN FLOW (CLASS 1,000)
Some degree of air cleanliness will be sacrificed toward the exhaust end of the room
Laminar flow clean rooms are the latest in clean room design and are second generation to conventional clean rooms
A higher level of cleanliness can be maintained in these type facilities because of the nature of laminar air flow patterns
Turbulent air flow patterns, which are characteristic of con- ventional clean rooms, tend to stir up and keep internally generated contamination in the air mass that lingers within the room
Laminar air flow patterns, which are essentially unidirectional as the air flows from the air supply to the exhaust, eliminate or substantially reduce the turbulent flow, and produce a cleaner air mass within the room
Laminar flow clean rooms have proven their worth in terms of economy and positive con- tamination control, and will be the only types constructed by the Air Force in the future
When the air moves from ceiling to floor with the floor serving as the return air grille, the room is called a down-flow or vertical laminar flow clean room
The down-flow design provides the greatest contamination control over the entire working area because airborne con- tamination is immediately carried down and out of the room as soon as it is generated
CHAPTER 7 LAMINAR FLOW CLEAN WORK STATIONS (CLASS 100 AND CLASS 1,000)
Air filtration must meet the par- ticle count of class 100 (ISO 14644-1 and ISO 14644-2) with no more than 100 particles per cubic foot of atmosphere 0
Laminar flow clean work stations offer a high degree of flexibility to operations which must be performed where clean rooms are not available or practical
In such situations the ambient contamination level of the room will be very high in comparison to the environment within the laminar flow work stations
Laminar flow work stations operate on the principle of removing contamination from the work space as soon as it is generated
It accomplishes this by blowing clean air from High Efficient Particulate Air (HEPA) filters over the work area at a velocity of 100 ft
The laminar air flow is unidirectional and carries contamination away from the work
Stag- gering equipment and personnel may also help in minimiz- ing the disruption of air flows
After all layouts are complete and equipment located, the air flow pattern must be checked
Figure 6-6 and Fig- ure 6-7 illustrate the emission patterns when personnel or objects are located in the air stream of cross-flow rooms
The size of dead air spaces and eddy currents depends on the size of objects deflecting the air stream
Equipment in a cross-flow room shall have the widest side parallel to the air stream

per_sentence.text: ronmental controls of a clean room, but which should be segregated from excessive dirt generation operation
Laminar Flow An airflow pattern in which the en- tire body of air within a confined area moves with uniform velocity along parallel lines
Laminar Flow Clean Room A clean room in which the laminar airflow characteristics predominate throughout the entire room, with a minimum of ed- dies
Laminar Flow Clean Work Station A clean work station in which the laminar airflow characteristics predominate throughout the entire airspace, with a minimum of eddies
CHAPTER 6 LAMINAR FLOW CLEAN ROOMS CROSS FLOW (CLASS 10,000) AND DOWN FLOW (CLASS 1,000)
Some degree of air cleanliness will be sacrificed toward the exhaust end of the room
Laminar flow clean rooms are the latest in clean room design and are second generation to conventional clean rooms
A higher level of cleanliness can be maintained in these type facilities because of the nature of laminar air flow patterns
Turbulent air flow patterns, which are characteristic of con- ventional clean rooms, tend to stir up and keep internally generated contamination in the air mass that lingers within the room

Laminar air flow patterns, which are essentially unidirectional as the air flows from the air supply to the exhaust, eliminate or substantially reduce the turbulent flow, and produce a cleaner air mass within the room
Laminar flow clean rooms have proven their worth in terms of economy and positive con- tamination control, and will be the only types constructed by the Air Force in the future
When the air moves from ceiling to floor with the floor serving as the return air grille, the room is called a down-flow or vertical laminar flow clean room

The down-flow design provides the greatest contamination control over the entire working area because airborne con- tamination is immediately carried down and out of the room as soon as it is generated
CHAPTER 7 LAMINAR FLOW CLEAN WORK STATIONS (CLASS 100 AND CLASS 1,000)
Air filtration must meet the par- ticle count of class 100 (ISO 14644-1 and ISO 14644-2) with no more than 100 particles per cubic foot of atmosphere 0
Laminar flow clean work stations offer a high degree of flexibility to operations which must be performed where clean rooms are not available or practical
In such situations the ambient contamination level of the room will be very high in comparison to the environment within the laminar flow work stations
Laminar flow work stations operate on the principle of removing contamination from the work space as soon as it is generated
It accomplishes this by blowing clean air from High Efficient Particulate Air (HEPA) filters over the work area at a velocity of 100 ft
The laminar air flow is unidirectional and carries contamination away from the work
Stag- gering equipment and personnel may also help in minimiz- ing the disruption of air flows
After all layouts are complete and equipment located, the air flow pattern must be checked
Figure 6-6 and Fig- ure 6-7 illustrate the emission patterns when personnel or objects are located in the air stream of cross-flow rooms
The size of dead air spaces and eddy currents depends on the size of objects deflecting the air stream
Equipment in a cross-flow room shall have the widest side parallel to the air stream

context_relevancy.query: Explain the key safety protocols for hot refueling Navy aircraft at Air Force bases as outlined in the technical manual.
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 1 SUPPORT EQUIPMENT FOR HOT REFUELING OPERATIONS
Only United States Air Force (USAP)-approved hot refueling equip- ment will be used
Do not hot refuel aircraft with a hot brake condition
Prior to hot refueling, all external tanks, stores, and weapons must be safed
The Base Fire Chief will determine the location that the standby Aircraft Rescue and Firefighting (ARFF) will be postured
Verify with the aircrew that fuel has not been dumped (jettisoned) on the previous flight
If fuel had been dumped and the position of the dump valve is not positively known to be closed, then aircraft will not be hot refueled
Aircraft high level shutoff valves must be pre-checked at the begin- ning of the hot refueling operation
During hot refueling, check that air is flowing from the aircraft fuel vent outlet(s) (except EF/F-18)
In a wartime situation, United States (US) Navy aircraft may land at United States Air Force (USAF) bases to refuel on its way to home base or carrier
The Navy aircraft may need to be hot refueled (i
This checklist provides the proce- dures for hot refueling Navy aircraft
The E/A-6, E-2, F-14, and S-3 aircraft will shut down the right engine for hot refueling
The EF/F-18 has been approved to hot refuel with both engines running
Although many details are present, this checklist is intended for use by fully qualified and trained hot refueling personnel only
Stop the aircraft at least 50 feet short of the hot refueling area
Signal the aircrew to taxi to the hot refueling area
Stop the aircraft at least 50 feet short of the hot refueling area
Signal the aircrew to taxi to the hot refueling area
Position the aircraft on the hot refueling pit
Many United States (US) Navy hot refueling operations employ the use of emergency dry-break disconnect devices for rapid taxi away in emergencies
USAF bases do not use this device, so NAVY pi- lots must be advised beforehand on current USAF hot refueling emergency procedures

context_relevancy.query: hydrostatic testing procedures aerospace systems
context_chunks:
Reinstall any removed components and leak test to maximum allowable operating pressure only with filtered dry gaseous nitrogen, Specification MIL-N-6011
4 Hydrostatic Test Procedure, Gaseous Systems
A careful engineering evaluation shall be made before attempting a hydrostatic test and the procedure shall be approved by cognizant safety personnel
Determine maximum allowable working pressure of system to be tested
Pressurize the system with the appropriate test fluid in increments of 50, 75, and 90 percent of the maximum allowable working pressure
Pressurize system to the maximum allowable working pressure of the test item
Release pressure and drain test fluid from system

The primary material being galvanized steel, the galvanizing will offer improved corrosion protection over the plain carbon steel

Types 321 and 409 stainless steel is weldable and will offer adequate corrosion protection
To prevent dissimilar metal related problems, do not use zinc plated or hot dipped zinc fasteners on stainless or corrosion resistant steel parts

context_relevancy.query: Are Aircraft Rescue and Firefighting vehicles required whenever passengers are present during fueling operations involving JP-4 or Jet B fuel?
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 1 SUPPORT EQUIPMENT FOR CONCURRENT REFUELING OPERATIONS
Aviation Hydrant Servicers (with passengers on board) or R5/R9/R11 refuelers with deadman controls and non-collapsible fuel servicing hose
Aircraft Rescue and Fire Fighting (ARFF) Vehicle (required only when servicing with JP-4 or Jet B fuel and passengers are on board)
Passenger Handling Equipment
Fleet Servicing and Catering Equipment (as required)
(b) *CSS shall determine if evacuation of passenger/team members from aircraft is required
(c) *Initiate normal or emergency evacuation of aircraft as necessary
(d) *If situation/hazard warrants, bring ARFF vehicle into ac- tion and turn operation over to ARFF personnel
(4) Ensure the Fire Department is notified at least 15 minutes before starting concurrent servicing operations, and, if person- nel are remaining on board the aircraft, informed of the num- ber of people involved
(5) Verify ARFF vehicle is present in the immediate area when the aircraft is being serviced with JP-4 or Jet B fuel and when passengers are on board
ARFF vehicle is not required when servicing is not with JP-4 or Jet B fuel or when passengers are not on board or for cargo aircraft
If personnel are remaining on board the aircraft, make sure the Chief Servicing Supervisor (CSS) informs the Fire Department of the number of people involved
With passengers/patients on board and servicing with JP-4 or Jet B fuel, a major aircraft rescue and fire fighting vehicle (P-2/19/23) will be positioned at the aircraft
Bond work stands to the aircraft when stands are used to access the aircraft servicing receptacles or support the fuel hose during servic- ing operations
Voice contact must be established and maintained at all times during the fuel servicing portion of concurrent servicing opera- tions when passengers, flight or ground crew members are on board the aircraft
The aircraft intercom system should be used as the primary means of maintaining voice contact between the fuel servicing team members
If the aircraft intercom system is inoperative and cannot be used to maintain voice communica- tions, portable hand-held radios may be used to provide voice contact subject to the following stipulations
Radios can be used within the FSSZ; however, only intrinsically safe radios can be used within 10 feet of any aircraft fuel vent outlet, fuel spill or fuel tank truck being filled from aircraft defueling
(5) Make sure ARFF vehicle is in place prior to starting the refu eling operation when servicing with JP-4 or Jet B fuel anc when passengers are on board
FUEL TRANSFER PHASE OF CONCURRENT SERVICING
(5) In those cases where on-scene fire protection is required, in the event of an In-Flight Emergency (IFE) or crash warning resulting in the departure of the on-scene fire vehicle, servicing operations already underway must be stopped
As a condition of continuance during IFEs, either an operational fixed, skid mounted, or portable Aqueous Film Forming Foam (AFFF) fire suppression system discharging through oscillating nozzles or at least one fully manned aircraft rescue and fire fighting vehicle must be positioned at the aircraft fuel servicing scene
Any type of clothing may be worn as outer garments when fuel servicing aircraft with high flash point fuels (JP-5 JP-8, JP-10, JET A, JET A-1, TS-1 below 82 oF, or diesel)
However, when fuel servicing aircraft with low flash point fuels (JP-4, JET B, AVGAS, or MOGAS), clothing containing more than 65 percent of any combination or mixture of nylon, rayon, wool or polyester shall not be worn as outer garments
These requirements also apply to draining air- craft fuel sumps

A typical cross-flow room is shown in Figure 6-1
Some degree of air cleanliness will be sacrificed toward the exhaust end of the room
Laminar flow clean rooms are the latest in clean room design and are second generation to conventional clean rooms
A higher level of cleanliness can be maintained in these type facilities because of the nature of laminar air flow patterns
Turbulent air flow patterns, which are characteristic of con- ventional clean rooms, tend to stir up and keep internally generated contamination in the air mass that lingers within the room
Laminar air flow patterns, which are essentially unidirectional as the air flows from the air supply to the exhaust, eliminate or substantially reduce the turbulent flow, and produce a cleaner air mass within the room
These types of clean rooms are generally classified as cross-flow (class 10,000) and down-flow (class 1,000), but are capable of be- ing maintained at class 100
When the air moves from ceiling to floor with the floor serving as the return air grille, the room is called a down-flow or vertical laminar flow clean room
The down-flow design provides the greatest contamination control over the entire working area because airborne con- tamination is immediately carried down and out of the room as soon as it is generated
per min) than the cross-flow room (100 ft

context_relevancy.query: aircraft ground equipment positioning guidelines GPU vs fuel truck placement
context_chunks:
Ensure aircraft is bonded to refueling equipment
Ground Power Unit (GPU) must be positioned 50 feet minimum, and to the maximum extent of the power cable, preferably upwind of fuel servicing area, and within 45 degrees either side of aircraft nose
If a GPU is not available and if carrier's manual so authorizes, aircraft Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) may be used dur- ing refueling
If APU is used during refueling, fuel truck must be positioned 50 feet from the APU exhaust out- let
(3) Ensure GPU is positioned and connected when re- quired
Ground Power Unit (GPU) must be positioned outside the fuel servicing zone and when possible, uphill of the fuel ser- vicing area, pressurized fuel servicing equipment, and fuel vents
(b) *Positioning and connecting GPU
2 Driving and Parking Fuel/Water Servicing Vehicle/Equipment
Fuel servicing vehicles will not be driven or parked closer than 25 feet from aircraft unless a spotter is used to direct the vehicle
(Exception: Fuel servicing vehicles being positioned for aircraft servicing may be driven under the horizontal stabilizers of AN-124, B-1, C-5, C-17, C-130, E-4, VC- 25, and Boeing 747 aircraft as long as: the vehicles stay on paved surfaces; maintain at least a ten foot clearance from any portion of the aircraft; and a spotter is used
) Special attention must be given to clearances between the fuel servicing vehicle and the aircraft cargo door when open and the ramp when lowered
) Under no circumstances will the vehicle be positioned closer than 10 feet from the aircraft
For AN-124, C-5, C-17, E-4B, VC-25, and B-747 aircraft, the fuel truck may be positioned as far under the wing as nec- essary for the fuel hose to reach the aircraft
Hydrant servicing vehicles and hydrant hose trucks with highlift platform servicing capability may be permitted under the wing of an air- craft, if this positioning is required to perform the fuel servicing operation
CHAPTER 9 REFUELING IN HARDENED AIRCRAFT SHELTERS (HAS), PROTECTIVE AIRCRAFT SHELTERS (PAS), ALERT SHELTERS AND FLOW-THROUGH REVETMENTS
This chapter provides precautions and restrictions when refueling aircraft inside a shelter or FTR using an R-9 or R-11 fuel truck
Positioning a truck near or inside a shelter can create significant space and clearance limitations
Fuel servicing vehicles can be positioned inside or outside of shelters
When positioned inside the shelters, trucks will be backed into the shelter on the right or left side of the aircraft
(3) Connected to a hangar electrical power source or ground power unit (GPU) that does not have GFCIs (ground fault circuit interrupters)
Use of air- craft chassis for current return means aircraft is structurally grounded through the power source cable and power source ground
Adding aircraft ground creates a ground in two places, a ground loop, and resultant fault

context_chunks:
AF Technical Order System
This manual describes physical and/or chemical processes which may cause injury or death to personnel, or damage to equipment, if not properly followed
This safety summary includes general safety precautions and instructions that must be understood and applied during operation and maintenance to ensure personnel safety and protection of equipment
All NFPA 1402-2007 Chapter 13 Outside Activities recommen- dations related to system and controls certification and label- ing by nationally recognized testing laboratories has been accomplished by the Air Force internal operational test and evaluation process (OT&E) conducted under the auspices of the independent Air Force Operational Test and Evaluation Center (AFOTEC)
Compliance with this Technical Order is required to retain the certification identified in NFPA 1402
Systems approved by AFCEC as complying with the requirements of this Technical Order are in compli- ance with NFPA 1403-2007 Live Fire Training Evolutions, Chapter 7 Exterior Props and Chapter 8 Exterior Class-B Fires
All NFPA 1403 elements related to system and con- trols certification by nationally recognized testing laborato- ries has been accomplished by the Air Force internal opera- tional test and evaluation process (OT& E) conducted under the auspices of the independent Air Force Operational Test and Evaluation Center (AFOTEC)
CHAPTER 9 INTERMEDIATE AND DEPOT REPAIR OF VEHICLES AND COMPONENTS

This Technical Order (TO) establishes United States Air Force (USAF) policy for the maintenance of aircraft fuel tanks/cells Included in this TO are general requirements for preparation of an aircraft for fuel systems maintenance and inspection
The requirements of this TO are applicable to all aircraft in the Air Force (AF) inventory
The TO is divided into the following Chapters: Responsibilities, Qualifications and Training; Safety, Health and Environmen- tal Requirements; Aircraft Fuel Systems Repair Facilities and Areas; Fuel Leak Evaluation, Classification and Temporary Repairs; Preparation for Maintenance; Integral Tanks; Air Logistics Complex (ALC) and Aerospace Maintenance and Regeneration Group (AMARG) Requirements; Equipment and Materials; and Fuel Cells
Summarizes the duties, qualifications and training for personnel and organizations involved in fuel systems repair, and provides for resolution when the requirements of this TO conflict with other direc- tives

Provides instructions for preparing an aircraft for fuel systems repair

The ATS will be a highly trained and qualified maintenance technician in a minimum grade of SSgt in accordance with Air Force Instruction (AFI) 21-101 and MAJCOM supplements

2 SAFETY SUMMARY
Various steps in this work package may require personnel to be exposed to extremely high temperatures and open flames
Make sure ignitor switches are wired to correct ignitors, solenoids, or pilot valves, if applicable
Ignition cable which is not MIL-3702, improper cable splices, transformer connections that are not made with lug/spade connectors, and ignitor connections which are not made with proper pigtails, will cause ignitor failures
Propane and water piping, and electrical conduits from the control stand to the burn area are located in an underground HDPE lined trench
Regulators in propane lines to mock-up burners are adjusted to increase or decrease flame size and set maxi- mum fire size for each burner
All burners are equipped with pneu- matically operated high-speed emergency shut off coaxial valves
3 Liquid Propane System
The liquid propane system supplies fuel for training exercises
Propane is supplied from a 10,000 water gallon or larger tank to the various burners in the burn area through a series of pipes and valves
Vapor pressure (Table 1) will vary with ambient temperature
Liquid propane has a boiling point of -44 Fahrenheit
A propane pump may be installed, in parallel to the main supply line, to obtain adequate pressure, if appli- cable
Safety features of the propane system are provided to stop the flow of fuel to the burners, and include a pneumatic emergency shutdown system, a manual shutdown system, and a seismic sensor shutdown system (if applicable)
Cathodic pro- tection is used to protect below ground piping from corrosion
Temperature and Pressure Relation sure relief valves are installed as required by National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) Standard 58

context_relevancy.query: List all safety precautions mentioned in the document that address the risks of fuel flammability and toxicity during aircraft refueling operations.
context_chunks:
Make sure refueling is not done within 300 feet of ground radar equipment
Make sure refueling is not done within 50 feet of other aircraft with engine(s) operating
Do not carry or wear loose metal objects, such as knives, keys, or other objects which might cause sparks
Fuel pressure from servicing equipment should not exceed 55 PSI, during normal refueling
Make sure aircraft is receiving correct fuel (JP-4, JP-5 or JP-8)
3 SAFETY PRECAUTIONS
General safety precautions apply regarding electrical storms, equipment discrepancies, spark producing items, grounding/bonding, clothing, dis- tance criteria, etc
Refueling team will consist of a minimum of seven (7) personnel
Telephone and radio landlines may be connected to the aircraft but not comnected/disconnettee during refueling
Passengers and NAOC personnel may stay on board, but are pro- hibited from exit/entry during fuel servicing
Severe electrical shock and burns may result when touching equipment or aircraft during High Frequency (HF) transmis- sions
Failure to comply could result in injury to, or death of, personnel or long term health hazards
Do not touch skin of aircraft or other metal objects; do not touch any portion of servicing vehicle
Hearing protection will be worn when aircraft engine is operating
Observe all refueling precautions
To prevent injury to personnel or damage to aircraft from pos- sible static electricity discharge, all personnel involved in refu- eling shall dissipate static potential by gripping grounding wire before starting refueling operation and repeating frequently during refueling operation
Static bond aircraft and refueling equipment
On FUEL control panel assembly, make sure WING dump switches are in NORM position
To prevent spillage, resulting in possible fire and/or explosion, make sure nozzle is in locked position
Make sure aircraft is receiving the correct fuel (JP-4, JP-5 or JP-8)
CHAPTER 2 SAFETY PRECAUTIONS
1 SAFETY PRECAUTIONS
The following safety precautions must be strictly complied with to pre- vent possible injury to personnel and damage to aircraft or equipment
All refueling and defueling operations will be accomplished under the supervision of the refueling supervisor
Clothing containing more than 65 percent of any combination or mixture of nylon, rayon, wool, or polyester will not be worn as outer garments in the FSSZ when fuel servicing aircraft with low flashpoint fuels (flashpoint 100 degrees Fahrenheit (ºF) or below)
Should a leak develop during refueling or defueling, stop all opera- tions
Necessary safety precautions will be taken and repairs ac- complished prior to resuming operations
(2) Within a 50 foot radius of operating aircraft, or with aircraft turned in such a way that engine exhaust is directed upon fueling/defueling operation
(6) When there are fuel leaks in the aircraft, fuel servicing equip- ment, or other equipment in the area
(8) If glowing or crackling fuel is noted when servicing the air- craft

context_relevancy.query: Summarize the key safety measures and operational considerations that distinguish hot refueling procedures for Navy aircraft at Air Force bases from standard refueling practices.
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 1 SUPPORT EQUIPMENT FOR HOT REFUELING OPERATIONS
Only United States Air Force (USAP)-approved hot refueling equip- ment will be used
Do not hot refuel aircraft with a hot brake condition
Prior to hot refueling, all external tanks, stores, and weapons must be safed
The Base Fire Chief will determine the location that the standby Aircraft Rescue and Firefighting (ARFF) will be postured
Verify with the aircrew that fuel has not been dumped (jettisoned) on the previous flight
If fuel had been dumped and the position of the dump valve is not positively known to be closed, then aircraft will not be hot refueled
Aircraft high level shutoff valves must be pre-checked at the begin- ning of the hot refueling operation
If an aircraft fails any pre- check, the aircraft can only be cold refueled
During hot refueling, check that air is flowing from the aircraft fuel vent outlet(s) (except EF/F-18)
Navy external tank vents are on the bottoms of the external tanks
In a wartime situation, United States (US) Navy aircraft may land at United States Air Force (USAF) bases to refuel on its way to home base or carrier
The Navy aircraft may need to be hot refueled (i
, refueled with an engine running) in order to minimize ground turnaround time and avoid the need for support equipment
This checklist provides the proce- dures for hot refueling Navy aircraft
The E/A-6, E-2, F-14, and S-3 aircraft will shut down the right engine for hot refueling
The EF/F-18 has been approved to hot refuel with both engines running
Although many details are present, this checklist is intended for use by fully qualified and trained hot refueling personnel only
During refueling, tank overpressure indicator lights on the fueling station door must be monitored to prevent rupture of the fuel tanks
Only EA-6 aircraft can be hot refueled at USAF bases
Stop the aircraft at least 50 feet short of the hot refueling area
Many United States (US) Navy hot refueling operations employ the use of emergency dry-break disconnect devices for rapid taxi away in emergencies
USAF bases do not use this device, so NAVY pi- lots must be advised beforehand on current USAF hot refueling emergency procedures
Stop the aircraft at least 50 feet short of the hot refueling area
Signal the aircrew to taxi to the hot refueling area
Position the aircraft on the hot refueling pit

Aerospace Ground Equipment (AGE) pressure systems contain gases or liquids under pressure and varying temperatures
2 OPERATING PRESSURES
Pressure systems safety precautions apply to all pressures in this range
The degree of hazard in pressure systems is proportional to the amount of energy stored, not the amount of pressure present
Therefore, low pressure, high volume systems can be as hazardous to personnel and equipment as high pressure systems of lower volumes
Since leaks can never be tolerated in pressure systems, all new, repaired, or modified systems or portions of systems shall be proof and leak tested prior to activation
Periodic testing of pressure systems shall be in accordance with the applicable system and/or equipment technical manuals

This checklist provides, in abbreviated form, procedures for con- current servicing of commercial "cargo only" aircraft, contracted to the Air Force Logistics Airlift (LOGAIR) and the Navy's QUICKTRANS systems
It is for use when performing concurrent servicing on Military installations only
Applicable aircraft are the Lockheed Hercules C-130 (L-100), Lockheed Electra (L-188), Douglas DC-9, and the Boeing 727
The requirements of this checklist must be ad- hered to when the concurrent servicing of applicable commercial aircraft is accomplished on the Air Force installation
Concurrent servic- ing is the servicing of fuel simultaneous with the performance of cargo off/onloading operations, power off nonfuel systems main- tenance, inspections, or fleet service
ERATORS, AND REFUELING PERSONNEL (REO AND LVM) AT THE AIRCRAFT

CHAPTER 5 CONCURRENT SERVICING OPERATIONS (CSO)
CONCURRENT SERVICING OF AIRCRAFT
On C/KC-135 (all models), Mobility Air Forces (MAF) and Commercial aircraft, a concurrent servicing System Safety Engineering Analysis (SSEA) is not required unless refueling/defueling with JP-4, OR loading/dovnloading munitions or explosives, OR servicing Liquid Oxygen (LOX) while performing maintenance
Concurrent servicing is defined as the simultaneous servicing of fuel or oxygen with either passengers on board or the performance of minor maintenance, fleet servicing, or baggage or cargo loading/unloading
Concurrent servicing primarily applies to commercial, contract, cargo, and passenger aircraft, but could apply to any aircraft undergoing fuel servicing with personnel on board or performance of minor maintenance
Refer to Chapter 6 for Integrated Combat Turnarounds (ICTs) (formerly CSOs Combat Sortie Generation) for the A-10, F-15, F-16, and F-22, and F-35 aircraft when munitions/ammunition loading/unloading or aircraft reconfiguration are involved
As long as the provisions of this chapter are followed, a SSEA is no longer required for concurrent servicing of aircraft with high flashpoint fuels
Fuel servicing and concurrent servicing of these aircraft will be done using approved checklists
Cargo containing explosives, oxygen or flammable gases or liquids shall not be loaded or unloaded during concurrent servicing operations unless otherwise noted in Table 5-1
A refueling supervisor (formerly Chief Servicing Supervisor (CSS) shall be provided by the USAF for all concurrent fuel servicing of contract airlift missions at military installations

Concurrent servicing is defined as the simultaneous servicing of fuel or oxygen with either passengers on board or the performance of minor maintenance, fleet servicing, or baggage or cargo loading/unloading

Concurrent servicing primarily applies to commercial, contract, cargo, and passenger aircraft, but could apply to any aircraft undergoing fuel servicing with personnel on board or performance of minor maintenance

context_relevancy.query: safety zone requirements shelters versus open-air refueling
context_chunks:
Servicing operations inside shelters/FTR present a greater degree of risk than the same operations conducted outside on open ramp
The fuel servicing safety zone criteria shall be complied with
When powered support equipment is re- quired for the fuel servicing operations, the equipment shall be positioned outside the shelter when possible
6 FUEL SERVICING SAFETY ZONE (FSSZ)
This is the area within 50 feet of a pressurized fuel carrying servicing component; i
The FSSZ applies to open ramps and to the insides of hangars and shelters that have been approved for fuel servicing operations
) The fuel servicing safety zone is established and maintained during pressurization and move- ment of fuel
See Figure 3-1 for an example of the bomber pit refueling safety zone and Figure 3-2 for an example of the fighter refueling safety zone
During fuel movement, active ignition sources shall be removed and kept out of the fuel servic- ing safety zone
Non-servicing vehicles and AGE Equipment (with engines not running) are allowed to be parked within the Fuel Servicing Safety Zone as long as they do not prevent the emergency egress of the servicing equipment or personnel
An aircraft adjacent to a fuel servicing safety zone can have its engines running as long as the aircraft thrust is not directed at the aircraft being refueled
Refueling units are sometimes filled on the flight line
The distance criteria estab- lished for aircraft refueling in Table 3-2 apply
8 SERVICING IN AIRCRAFT ALERT, HARDENED/PROTECTIVE AIRCRAFT SHELTERS (HAS/PAS), OR FLOW THROUGH REVETMENTS (FTRS)
Open Hardened/ Protective Aircraft Shelter doors
When servicing in aircraft alert, Hardened/Protective aircraft shelters, or FTRs, the following applies:
Servicing operations inside shelters/FTRs present a greater degree of risk than the same operations con- ducted outside on open ramp
The adjacent aircraft parking criteria (Paragraph 3
The fuel servicing safety zone criteria shall be complied with
In the case of existing shelters and FTRs that do not meet these criteria, required shelter/FTR electrical systems must be activated prior to refuel/defuel operations and left un- changed
Refueler vehicle engine should not be operated more than 20 minutes when aircraft entry doors are closed
When aircraft entry doors are closed, the shelter aircraft can be refueled inside a completely closed shelter
Crew members conducting in-shelter refueling with shelter doors completely closed should be limited to four per duty day and should have at least a sixty minute period of low or no fuel vapor exposure between refueling aircraft in a closed shelter
Shelter doors will remain open during fuel servicing
(3) Fuel servicing equipment is inside the shelter
Fuel servicing vehicles will not be backed into shelters until a chock is placed to stop the vehicle in case of brake failure and a spotter is in position to direct movement
9 UNIQUE REQUIREMENTS WHEN SERVICING IN HARDENED/PROTECTIVE AIRCRAFT SHELTERS
CHAPTER 9 REFUELING IN HARDENED AIRCRAFT SHELTERS (HAS), PROTECTIVE AIRCRAFT SHELTERS (PAS), ALERT SHELTERS AND FLOW-THROUGH REVETMENTS
This chapter provides precautions and restrictions when refueling aircraft inside a shelter or FTR using an R-9 or R-11 fuel truck
Positioning a truck near or inside a shelter can create significant space and clearance limitations
In most cases, the truck is outside the shelter or FTR, but in some critical wartime or training scenarios, it is necessary to place the truck inside the shelter or FTR, and, in the case of a HAS/PAS, with the shelter doors closed
The general and emergency aircraft refueling proce- dures in Chapters 1 and 2 will apply to shelter/FTR operations
Fuel servicing vehicles can be positioned inside or outside of shelters
Except during nose-in or double-stuff conditions, the nearest part of the fuel-servicing vehicle must not be closer then three feet from the shelter wall or door

context_relevancy.query: How does the integration of standardized procedures in military technical manuals impact the coordination between different branches during joint operations?
context_chunks:
The purpose of this manual is to provide Vehicle Fleet Managers (VFM), Vehicle Management Superintendents (VMS), supervisors and technicians a single publication encompassing technical and managerial guidance related to the maintenance and upkeep of their respective vehicle and vehicular equipment fleets
This technical manual specifies procedures, materials, and guidance for the VFM
Corrosion Prevention and Control For Air Force Vehicles
Intermediate and Depot Repair of Vehicles and Components
Use this manual in conjunction with and in support of the appropriate Army Technical Manuals, Technical Bulletins (TBs), Air Force Instruc- tions (AFI), Air Force (AF) Manuals, and Air Force Technical Orders (AFTOs)
Where this manual conflicts with Army and AF vehicle specifi technical manuals/Technical Orders (TOs), except in regards to Preventative Maintenance intervals, the vehicle specific guidance shall take precedence over this manual
This chapter is intended for use by depot and field level maintenance personnel as a guide for proper corrosion prevention and control of Ground Communications, Electronics, and Meteorological (C-E-M) equipment during storage and shipment
Military Specifications, Standards, and Manuals
All activities involved with corrosion prevention and control of C-E-M equipmen during storage and shipment shall use this technical manual in conjunction with Technical Order (TO) 1-1-8, TO 1-1-689-series, TO 1-1-691, AFPAM (I) 24-237 and equipment system specific maintenance manuals as a guide for corrosion prevention and control
Numerous mili- tary specifications, standards, and manuals are mentioned throughout this chapter and should be referred to as directed
Specifying the level of protection provides the level of military preservation and packing that a given item requires to ensure that it is not de- graded during shipment and storage
1 Military Level of Preservation
Military Level of Packing
ICTs provide units operational flexibility in managing resources and a rrpiid means of generating mission capable aircraft
ICT procedures may be used during actual contingencies, scheduled exercises, and daily flying operations to enhance aircraft availability during combat operations
Refer to AFI 21-101 and MAJCOM supplements for additional guidance
Integrated Combat Turnaround (ICT)
This operation was originally con- ceived to only be performed under emergency combat conditions
Today, ICT operations may be performed as part of normal day-to-day training exercises to maintain proficiency for such contingencies
Hot Integrated Combat Turnaround (Hot ICT)
Hot ICTs are approved for combat operations, combat training, exercises and evaluations as authorized by the major command
This does not restrict simulated training operations as outlined in the modified procedures for the appropriate weapon system
This manual may be used at other locations to perform AMARG processes and procedures with owning agency approval
Instructions contained in other related specific directives will be used in conjunction with this manual
When conflicts exists between applicable technical data and this manual, this manual shall take precedence except under the following conditions:
When the standardized procedures of this manual may damage equipment
When detailed procedures are specific to the aircraft or engine model and specified in the applicable Work Load Agreement (WLA)/Statement of Work (SOW)
4 COMPLIANCE TERMINOLOGY
For the purpose of this technical order, the following definitions apply:
Many tasks must be accomplished using the applicable technical data for the aircraft
Examples of applicable technical data are Technical Orders, Maintenance Instruction Manuals, Technical Manuals, Electronic Technical Data, Process Orders, Engineering Orders, Requests for Engineering Instructions, 107/202 dispositions, Special Packaging Instructions (SPI), approved drawings, Sealing Diagrams, and Module Worksheets
Deviations from applicable technical data will be accom- plished per owning agency guidelines
Standard abbreviations are in accordance with ASME Y14
Air Force Technical Order

Since the fires are intended to simulate a solid object fire event, the following actions are in- appropriate and constitute an emergency condi- tion requiring activation of the system E-Stop switch for emergency shutdown anytime the nozzle or any firefighter including his PPE: (1) breaks the vertical plane of the cargo box, (2) breaks the plane of the control surfaces in the cockpit, (3) breaks the plane of any passenger fire burner shield or the area above the burner itself, (4) breaks either end plane of an engine, (5) enters the area under the cockpit stairs, (6) enters the area under the passenger seats or breaks the plane of the front surface of the pas- senger seat backs, (7) enters an area above an operating burner, (8) or enters the area directly below the APU or Wheel fire when the burner is operating

1 Air Frame and Hightail Fire
Frame and Hightail fire training exercises are as follows:
Extremely high temperatures and hot surfaces will be encountered during the training exer- cise

Since the fires are intended to simulate a solid object fire event, the following actions are in- appropriate and constitute an emergency condi- tion requiring activation of the system E-Stop switch for emergency shutdown anytime the nozzle or any firefighter including his PPE: breaks the vertical plane of the cargo box; breaks the plane of the control surfaces in the cockpit; breaks the plane of any passenger fire burner shield or the area above the burner it- self; breaks either end plane of an engine; en- ters the area under the cockpit stairs; enters the area under the passenger seats or breaks the plane of the front surface of the passenger seat backs; enters an area above an operating burner; or enters the area directly below the APU or Wheel fire when the burner is operating

Since the fires are intended to simulate a solid object fire event, the following actions are in- appropriate and constitute an emergency condi- tion requiring activation of the system E-Stop switch for emergency shutdown anytime the nozzle or any firefighter including his PPE: (1) breaks the vertical plane of the cargo box, (2) breaks the plane of the control surfaces in the cockpit, (3) breaks the plane of any passenger fire burner shield or the area above the burner itself, (4) breaks either end plane of an engine, (5) enters the area under the cockpit stairs, (6) enters the area under the passenger seats or breaks the plane of the front surface of the pas- senger seat backs, (7) enters an area above an operating burner, (8) or enters the area directly below the APU or Wheel fire when the burner is operating
Pre-burn time will not exceed 3 minutes (180 sec- onds)

8 FIRE PROTECTION REQUIREMENTS
Fire protection requirements for concurrent servicing are:
At least a 15 minute notification from Maintenance Operation Center (MOC) or the Command Post to the fire depart- ment prior to starting concurrent servicing operations is required to allow aircraft rescue and fire fighting vehicle positioning for a three minute response time
If hazardous cargo is involved, the fire department will also be notified of its type and quantity
If passengers/patients are on board, the number of passengers/patients will be given to the fire department
(b) *CSS shall determine if evacuation of passenger/team members from aircraft is required
(d) *If situation/hazard warrants, bring ARFF vehicle into ac- tion and turn operation over to ARFF personnel
(4) Ensure the Fire Department is notified at least 15 minutes before starting concurrent servicing operations, and, if person- nel are remaining on board the aircraft, informed of the num- ber of people involved
(5) Verify ARFF vehicle is present in the immediate area when the aircraft is being serviced with JP-4 or Jet B fuel and when passengers are on board
However, Fire Department should be notified of impending concurrent servicing
Make sure the Concurrent Servicing Supervisor notifies the Fire Department 15 minutes prior to performing concurrent servicing operations
If personnel are remaining on board the aircraft, make sure the Chief Servicing Supervisor (CSS) informs the Fire Department of the number of people involved

Make sure the Chief Servicing Supervisor (CSS) notifies the Fire Department at least 15 minutes prior to performing concurrent ser- vicing operations

context_relevancy.query: How do the inspection protocols for electrical insulation materials differ from those for structural metal components in safety-critical environments?
context_chunks:
1 Plastic parts shall be free of cracks, breaks, chips, scratches or other severe damage that may impair their proper function or present a personnel health/safety hazard
3 Ceramic insulating materials will be free of surface cracks or glazing
Minor chips are acceptable providing the electrical insulation properties and function is not impaired or present a personnel health/safety hazard
5 Electrical wires and cables having plastic jacket insulation shall not be painted or coated with greases or oils except as required for protection from moisture and fungus
Rubber materials and parts shall be inspected on a frequent periodic basis for damage and deterioration
Also, insects can feed on electrical insulation, varnishes, and circuit board coat- ings
Many common assembly, maintenance, or repair processes produce corro- sive by-products
If adequate ventilation is not present, these fumes can cause or accelerate the corrosion of eleccronic components
Residues from solder fluxes can damage electronics by:
Producing corrosive vapors that settle on adjacent components
Attacking insulation, reducing its effectiveness
Changing the electrical resistance of soldered joints
Equipment removed from its housings for maintenance and repair can become exposed to environments from which it is normally protected
Proper use of protective covers and shipping containers is essential to preventing and minimizing corrosion damage
Use of proper materials for packaging is essential for the prevention of corrosion damage
Some glues, paints, varnishes, resins, and preservatives can emit corrosive vapors
Effects of Corrosion on Electronic Equipment |
| Power cables | Disintegration of insulation, wire and connector deterioration, shorts |
| Waveguides | Loss of integrity against moisture, pitting, reduction of efficiency, structural deteriora- tion |
| Printed circuit boards | Shorts, increased resistance, component and system failures |
| Bus bars | Structural and electrical failures |
2 Recognizing Corrosion
The ability to recognize corrosion is essential to performing a comprehensive corrosion inspection
In most cases, deterioration of nonmetallic components allows moisture intrusion
Table 8-3 describes the type of attack and the appearance of the deterioration for common nonmetallic items in Communications, Electronics, and Meteorological (C-E-M) equipment
specific information regarding hazards to per- sonnel during cleanup operations involving these materials and the required PPE
Failure to observe these procedures may result in electrical short circuits and fires
Aircraft, missile, or equipment constructed utiliz- ing graphite, boron, or tungsten fiber composite materials in contact with metal structures or sub- structures create a high potential for establishing galvanic corrosion cells
This can result in corro- sion of the metal components if the structure is exposed to an electrolyte medium, such as salt water
Since graphite, boron, and tungsten are very conductive, these fibers can damage unpro- tected avionic, electronic, and electrical equipment within several miles of the incident site by settling on and shorting out electrical contacts

context_relevancy.query: What two standards regulate insulating matting requirements for high-voltage zones in military aviation contexts?
context_chunks:
CONTROL AND USE OF INSULATING MATTING FOR HIGH-VOLTAGE APPLICATION
Insulating Matting As used in this techni- cal manual is rubber matting manufactured to withstand a maximum of 3000 volts of electricity to ground
High Voltage As used in this technical manual applies to voltage of 600 to 3000 volts
The Ground Safety Officer shall inspect areas where insulating matting is required
The Ground Safety Officer will determine if insulating is required and approve or disapprove the requisi- tions
This technical manual provides information and instructions for the control and use of insulating matting meeting Specification MIL-M-15562 and AFOSH 91-50
This technical manual applies to all Air Force activ- ities and contractors if obtaining insulating matting as Government Furnished Equipment
It is not necessary for the Ground Safety Officer to justify use on insulating matting
Insulating matting shall be used for person- nel protection in areas where personnel are subject to high voltage
Typical examples of areas where insulating matting is required are:
Insulating matting shall not be used for non-skid applications, such as walkways, hall runners, in front of work benches (non-electrical) or on work and test benches, etc
For these applications general purpose matting shall be used
No certifica- tion for use of general purpose matting is required
Insulating matting shall be replaced when worn to one-half original thickness
Embedded metallic chips and deterioration caused by oil or ultraviolet rays will affect the safety of the insulating matting
Insulating matting is for protection
This matting shall not be depended upon for protection when handling hot wires and circuits
Generally, only one run or strip of insulating matting shall be used on floor around high voltage areas
If more than one run or strip of matting is required, the Ground Safety Officer will determine the amount required
Resistance or Conductance of Insulating Materials
ASTM-D4701 Methylene Chloride, Technical Grade
IPC-4101 (Series) Specification for Base Materials for Rigid and Multilayer Printed Boards
IPC-A-610 (Series) Acceptability for Electronic Assemblies
IPC/EIA J-STD-001 (Series) Requirements for Soldered Electrical and Electronic Assemblies
IPC/JEDEC J-STD-033 Handling, Packing, Shipping and Use of Moisture/Reflow Sensitive Surface Mount Devices
MIL-DTL-81997 (Series) Pouches, Cushioned, Flexible, Electrostatic-Free Reclosable,
MIL-1-46058 (Series) Insulating Compound, Electrical
MIL-PRF-81705 (Series) Barrier Materials, Flexible, Electrostatic Protective, Heat Sealable
MIL-STD-129 (Series) Military Marking for Shipment and Storage
MIL-STD-1686 (Series) Electrostatic Discharge Control Program for Protection of Electrical and Electronic Parts, Assemblies and Equipment (Excluding Electrically Initiated Explosive Devices)
When specified, apply insulating electrical varnish which meets Specification MIL-I-24092, to points in power sup- plies exposed to high voltage that could cause high altitude arcing
Varnish-type coatings are to be used only when required by equipment specification or by other official directives
If in doubt about the safety of the insulating matting, the Ground Safety Officer should be asked to inspect the insulating matting to determine if it should be replaced
Insulating matting that is removed because of unsafe conditions may be used any place or any way approved by local offices

context_relevancy.query: How do technical manuals balance procedural consistency with operational flexibility across different military domains while maintaining compliance with standardized protocols?
context_chunks:
INTERACTIVE ELECTRONIC TECHNICAL MANUAL An "information oriented", digital technical manual whose format and style are optimized for computer presentation
IETM organization facilitates easy user access to technical information while the display device pro- vides interactive procedural guidance, navigational directions, and supplemental information
An IETM facilitates the interchange of mainte- nance manual information with logistic support data supplemental to maintenance, such as maintenance data collection, training documentation supply interface and data presentation control
1 DESCRIPTION OF MANUAL
Procedures contained herein are considered mandatory and no deviation is permitted
Primary importance of this technical manual is to make sure that safety and rescue equipment outlined herein is safe for accomplishment of tasks for which they were initially designed
Comments and recommendations for improvements to this manual will be submitted in accor- dance with Technical Order (TO) 00-5-1
The provisions of this TO are applicable to all commands and separate operating agencies of the United States Air Force (USAF), Air Force (AF) Reserve, and the Air National Guard (ANG)
This chapter is intended for use by depot and field level maintenance personnel as a guide for proper corrosion prevention and control of Ground Communications, Electronics, and Meteorological (C-E-M) equipment during storage and shipment
Military Specifications, Standards, and Manuals
All activities involved with corrosion prevention and control of C-E-M equipmen during storage and shipment shall use this technical manual in conjunction with Technical Order (TO) 1-1-8, TO 1-1-689-series, TO 1-1-691, AFPAM (I) 24-237 and equipment system specific maintenance manuals as a guide for corrosion prevention and control
Numerous mili- tary specifications, standards, and manuals are mentioned throughout this chapter and should be referred to as directed
Specifying the level of protection provides the level of military preservation and packing that a given item requires to ensure that it is not de- graded during shipment and storage
1 Military Level of Preservation
Military Level of Packing
Manuals, Technical Time Compliance Technical Orders (TCTO) and TCTO Supplements
General Style and Format Requirements for Technical Manuals
Contract Data Requirements List (CDRL)
Technical Manual (TM) Change Recommendation and Reply
Computation of Technical Order Reading Grade Level
Technical Order Publication Change Request
Maintenance Discrepancy and Work Document
This manual may be used at other locations to perform AMARG processes and procedures with owning agency approval
When conflicts exists between applicable technical data and this manual, this manual shall take precedence except under the following conditions:
When the standardized procedures of this manual may damage equipment
When detailed procedures are specific to the aircraft or engine model and specified in the applicable Work Load Agreement (WLA)/Statement of Work (SOW)
4 COMPLIANCE TERMINOLOGY
For the purpose of this technical order, the following definitions apply:
5 APPLICABLE TECHNICAL DATA

per_sentence.text: INTERACTIVE ELECTRONIC TECHNICAL MANUAL An "information oriented", digital technical manual whose format and style are optimized for computer presentation
IETM organization facilitates easy user access to technical information while the display device pro- vides interactive procedural guidance, navigational directions, and supplemental information

An IETM facilitates the interchange of mainte- nance manual information with logistic support data supplemental to maintenance, such as maintenance data collection, training documentation supply interface and data presentation control
1 DESCRIPTION OF MANUAL
Procedures contained herein are considered mandatory and no deviation is permitted
Primary importance of this technical manual is to make sure that safety and rescue equipment outlined herein is safe for accomplishment of tasks for which they were initially designed
Comments and recommendations for improvements to this manual will be submitted in accor- dance with Technical Order (TO) 00-5-1
The provisions of this TO are applicable to all commands and separate operating agencies of the United States Air Force (USAF), Air Force (AF) Reserve, and the Air National Guard (ANG)

This chapter is intended for use by depot and field level maintenance personnel as a guide for proper corrosion prevention and control of Ground Communications, Electronics, and Meteorological (C-E-M) equipment during storage and shipment

Numerous mili- tary specifications, standards, and manuals are mentioned throughout this chapter and should be referred to as directed

context_relevancy.query: minimum wing tip separation distance during rapid defueling with fire truck presence
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 7 KC-10; KC/EC/RC-135 RAPID DEFUELING
RAPID DEFUELING EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN PROCEDURES
Ensure sufficient space is available to hold fuel being defueled from the aircraft
Due to the fire hazards associated with rapid defueling op- erations, the preferred distance between aircraft wing tips is 50 feet
At installations where aircraft parking space is lim- ited and hydrant fuel pit spacing will not permit 50 foot aircraft wing tip clearance, the wing tip separation can be reduced to a minimum of 35 feet
However, whenever a distance of less than 50 feet (wing tip to wing tip) is main- tained, an aircraft rescue and firefighting vehicle must be at the aircraft during rapid defueling operations
CHAPTER 4 KC-10/135 HOT DEFUELING
Due to the fire hazards associated with hot defueling opera- tions, the preferred distance between aircraft wingtips is 50 feet
Whenever a distance of less than 50 feet is maintained between aircraft, wing tips a crash fire rescue vehicle must be at the aircraft during hot defueling operations
Stop 25 or more feet from aircraft
C-130, KC-135, KC-46, and EC/RC-135 aircraft have been approved for wet wing defueling
At installations where aircraft parking space is limited and hydrant fuel pit spacing will not permit 50-foot aircraft wing tip clearance, the (aircraft to aircraft) wing tip separation can be reduced to a minimum of 35 feet
However, whenever a distance of less than 50 feet (wing tip to wing tip) is maintained an aircraft rescue and fire fighting vehicle will be on standby posture per Chapter 3
Should a malfunction occur, the wet wing defueling operation will be stopped immediately
For E-4B aircraft, the distance from the wingtip can be reduced to 100 feet
For B-2A aircraft home station hot pit refuel ing only, the distance from wingtip vent FSSZ to uninhabitated buildings can be reduced to 55 feet
Part of an aircraft may pass within 50 feet of a hot refueling operation as long as the operating engine(s) of the taxiing aircraft do not penetrate the 50-foot criteria
When rapid defueling with an external hydraulic power cart (and no aircraft engine operating) this distance can be re- duced to 100 feet
Bomber Refueling Safety Zone Example
MINIUUM 10 SEPARATION WINGTIP SEPARATION HOSE CART A-2 WATER TRUCK POSITIONING 25 FT FROM VENTS

context_relevancy.query: aircraft fire training facility emergency shutoff vs fuel servicing grounding requirements
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 4 REFUELING/DEFUELING PREPARATIONS
Aircraft flaps must be in full up position on aircraft requiring under wing fuel servicing prior to positioning servicing ve- hicle
Engines may be shutoff while using the PAHT-750-8 HSV; however, the igni- tion must be in the Accessory (ACC) position while refueling
Ground servicing vehicle to static ground in accordance with Tech- nical Order (TO) 00-25-172 and Aircraft -2 checklist
Attend briefing by Aircraft Servicing Supervisor concerning amount of fuel to be refueled/defueled and procedure to be followed in case of emergency
EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN PROCEDURES
During aircraft servicing, the refueling unit operator will monitor the refueler for fuel leaks, ignition sources and other indications of a possible malfunction
Notify aircraft servicing supervisor, operations expediter and Fuels Control Center (FCC), if possible
Evacuate area if instructed by servicing supervisor or fire depart- ment personnel
In case of fuel spill other than normal aircraft venting, do not evacuate fuel servicing vehicle until area is declared safe by fire department personnel
In the event of an emergency, shutdown the refueling unit as follows:
Notify aircraft fuel servicing supervisor and Fuels Control Center
Evacuate area, if instructed by fuel servicing supervisor or fire de partment personnel
Fire department aircraft fire training facility
Fire department aircraft training facility, radios, vehicle apparatus and Personal Protec- tive Equipment (PPE)
Hot refueling and pressurized fuel servicing operations require installed fire suppression systems which control fuel spill fires and mitigated personnel and aircraft exposure by removing spilled fuel from the structure
3 Fire Department Standby Requirements
Table 3-1 lists various fire protection requirements for Aircraft Rescue and Fire Fighting (ARFF) vehicles
Due to a limited amount of ARFF equipment available, on occasion the Senior Fire Officer (SFO) may be required to direct standby vehicles to other aircraft emergencies or position ARFF vehicles to cover multiple hot refuelings
No fuel servicing portion of a new CSO/ICT may begin unless authorized by the wing commander (or senior local operational commander) until the ARFF vehicle has returned to a standby posture
Additionally, one wheeled flightline fire extinguisher must be located within 100 feet of the aircraft
Servicing personnel shall be trained in the use of this extinguisher

6 FACTORS INFLUENCING CORROSION
Both natural and man-made environments cause corrosion of SE
Natural conditions that affect the corrosion process are moisture, temperature, salt atmospheres, ozone, sand, dust, solar radiation, insects and birds, and microorganisms
Man-made conditions that affect the corrosion process are industrial pollution, manufacturing operations, storage conditions, and shipment
Factors that influence metal corrosion and the rate at which it occurs are outlined in Figure 1-2
Cadmium, nickel, chromium, and silver are sometimes used as protective plating
Metals have a wide range of corrosion resistance

2 Dissimilar Metal Coupling (Galvanic Corrosion)

14 VEHICLE CLEANING AND CORROSION PROTECTION

Climate/humidity controlled storage facilities greatly enhance corrosion prevention/control programs and increase the likelihood of operating within this waiver

| 6.16 FACTORS INFLUENCING CORROSION. |  |
Factors that influence metal corrosion and the rate at which it occurs are outlined in this section. Both natural and man-made environments cause corrosion of vehicles. Natural conditions that affect the corrosion process are moisture, temperature, salt atmospheres, ozone, sand, dust, solar radiation, insects and birds, and microorganisms. Man-made conditions that affect the corrosion process are industrial pollution, manufac- turing operations, storage conditions, and shipment. By understanding these conditions, maintenance personnel will be better able to prevent corrosion from initially occurring or minimize the impact of corrosion after it occurs.
6.16.1 Moisture. Moisture is present in air as gas (water vapor) or as finely divided droplets of liquid (mist or fog) and often contains contaminants (such as chlorides, sulfates, and nitrates) that increase its corrosive effects. Moisture will enter all areas of vehicles that air can enter. All enclosed areas, that are not hermetically sealed, allow air to enter and leave as the difference in pressure between the inside and outside the equipment changes. These pressure differences occur when atmospheric pressure changes and when the air temperature inside an enclosed area changes. As the surrounding surfaces are heated, moisture-laden air is drawn into the enclosed area or absorbed in open cell foam As the air around the equipment cools, moisture will condense in the enclosed areas and settle in the lowest area of the part.

Each installation shall main- tain a record of all maintenance and inspection actions con- ducted on the AFTF
2 Semi-Annual System Safety Inspection and Op- erational Checks
The Semi-Annual System Safety Inspec- tion and Operational Checks (WP 006 00) is an integral ele- ment of the general maintenance procedures of the AFTF
In the interest of safety and to maintain AFTFs in a manner that will ensure its serviceability and prevent deterioration in op- erational effectiveness, installations will make sure all main- tenance and repair work shall comply with Air Force Tech- nical Order 35E1-2-13-1 Operation and Maintenance Instructions for AFTFs, manufacturer's recommendations and procedures, and local, state, and federal guidelines as applicable to the work involved
AFCEC/ CXF will determine if the AFTF will remain out-of-service and if a site visit is necessary
In the interest of safety and to maintain AFTFs in a manner that will ensure its ser- viceability and prevent deterioration in operational effective- ness, installations will make sure all maintenance and repair work shall comply with AFTO 35E1-2-13-1 Operation and Maintenance Instructions for AFTFs, manufacturer's recom- mendations and procedures, and local, state, and federal guidelines as applicable to the work involved
AFCEC/CXF will determine if the AFTF will remain out-of-service and if a site visit is necessary
Periodic reviews of the AFTFs will be conducted by AFCEC/ CXF to determine the effectiveness of the semi-annual in- spection procedures
If the semi-annual inspection is not conducted, within 240 days, then the AFTF will be put out of service until the inspection is completed and documented
3 SEMI-ANNUAL SYSTEM SAFETY INSPECTION AND OPERATIONAL CHECKS
Reliability has always been a major problem in pressure systems because of the nature of the materials being handled and the stringent operational requirements imposed on many of the components
Reliability in many cases does not mean that an item must function properly over and over again, but that it must work the one and only time its ultimate function is required
1 The testing of one component, or a group of components, is not sufficient to assure system reliability
Quality control of all components must be maintained during manufacture
Preventive maintenance is another major step towards increased reliability
Scheduled maintenance of system components has proven effective towards increasing the reliability of pressure systems
14 SYSTEM CLEANLINESS
System cleanliness is a major problem in pressure systems
Systems are usually cleaned component by component because of the many problems which are encountered in cleaning the great variety o components in a pressure system
Extreme care must be exercised to maintain system cleanliness during the assembly installation, and testing of these systems

context_relevancy.query: How do Immediate Action and Routine Action TCTO categories differ in terms of compliance timelines and safety risk implications for Air Force operations?
context_chunks:
1 Immediate Action TCTOs
The compliance paragraph of the TCTO will state that the TCTO will be accomplished immediately (Paragraph 7
2 Urgent Action TCTOs
The compliance paragraph will state that the TCTO will be accomplished within a specified time (from to 10 days) after receipt of the TCTO and kits, when kits are required (Table 7-1)
This policy provides advance notice of the safety implications of the TCTO and maintenance scheduling information prior to kit availability
When Immediate or Urgent Action TCTOs do not require kits or parts, affected TOs will be updated by issuance of Interim Opera- tional and Safety Supplements IAW TO 00-5-1
3 Routine Action TCTOs
There are three levels of priority for the seven types TCTOs:: Immediate Action, Urgent Action, and Routine Action
The priority of a published TCTO may be changed by is- suance of a TCTO supplement or replacement TCTO
The action requires approval at the same level as the original TCTO approval au- thority
Immediate Action TCTOs are only issued with approval of the Lead MAJCOM to prevent use of equipment or pro- cedures until hazardous safety conditions, which pose an unacceptable risk of fatality or serious injury to personnel or extensive damage to or destruction of valuable property, can be corrected
1 The urgency of Immediate Action TCTOs requires rapid (immediate) action to remove the aircraft from service, prevent launch of missiles, discontinue operation of ground Communication-Elecrrniii (C-E) systems, or use of related support equipment, personal equipment or munitions
When possible, corrective actions are included in Immediate Action TCTOs
3 Any Immediate Action TCTO that will result in partial or complete weapon system removal from operational availability requires the issue of an official notification through the Advance Notification System (ANS) (Chapter 9)
Urgent Action TCTOs are issued when potentially hazardous safety conditions could result in injury to personnel, dam age to property, or when conditions cause unacceptable reductions in combat efficiency
Urgent Action TCTOs may be issued as a formal or an ITCTO
1 The urgency of these TCTOs requires compliance within specified time limits, which cannot be exceeded without an applicable risk acceptance decision
If compliance is not accomplished by expiration of the time limit, Urgent Action TCTOs require action to remove air- craft from service, discontinue use of air-launched missiles, prevent launch of missiles, discontinue operation of ground C-E equipment, or use of Support Equipment (SE), personal equipment, materials or munitions
Routine Action TCTOs are issued for any conditions not covered under Immediate or Urgent Action TCTOs
Routine Action TCTOs may be issued as a formal or an ITCTO (see Figure 2-3)
2 Routine Action TCTOs may also provide enhancements to equipment or system capabilities
ITCTOs are issued as either Immediate Action, Urgent Action, Routine Safety or Record with compliance periods as follows:
1 Immediate Action ITCTO
Compliance period is immediate
4 Record TCTOs for ICBM and Associated SE
Record TCTOs for ICBM and associated SE must meet the following criteria prior to issue:
5 Routine TCTOs for ICBM and Associated SE
7 Interim TCTO Supplements
3 TIME COMPLIANCE TECHNICAL ORDERS
IAW AFI 23-101, TCTOs are intended to expedite the accomplishment of retrofit changes to end articles/items, parts, and materiel within spe- cific time periods and reduce the probability of accidents/urreliabllit of systems or equipment due to non-compliance
TCTOs provide instruc- tions to military system End Items as well as aseembies/components that are installed into them
TCTOs are used to modify weapon systems, commodities, and equipment necessary to support the weapon system within specified time limits, initiate special one-time inspection to impose temporary restrictions and track support system and equipment configuration on systems or equipment
1 TCTO Types, Priorities, and Levels
One of three priorities is assigned to the TCTO; Immediate Action, Urgent Action, or Routine Action
4 LIFE CYCLE MANAGEMENT OF TCTOS

If compliance is not accomplished by expiration of the time limit, Urgent Action TCTOs require action to remove air- craft from service, discontinue use of air-launched missiles, prevent launch of missiles, discontinue operation of ground C-E equipment, or use of Support Equipment (SE), personal equipment, materials or munitions
Routine Action TCTOs are issued for any conditions not covered under Immediate or Urgent Action TCTOs
Routine Action TCTOs may be issued as a formal or an ITCTO (see Figure 2-3)
2 Routine Action TCTOs may also provide enhancements to equipment or system capabilities
ITCTOs are issued as either Immediate Action, Urgent Action, Routine Safety or Record with compliance periods as follows:
1 Immediate Action ITCTO
Compliance period is immediate
4 Record TCTOs for ICBM and Associated SE
Record TCTOs for ICBM and associated SE must meet the following criteria prior to issue:
5 Routine TCTOs for ICBM and Associated SE
7 Interim TCTO Supplements
3 TIME COMPLIANCE TECHNICAL ORDERS

IAW AFI 23-101, TCTOs are intended to expedite the accomplishment of retrofit changes to end articles/items, parts, and materiel within spe- cific time periods and reduce the probability of accidents/urreliabllit of systems or equipment due to non-compliance
TCTOs provide instruc- tions to military system End Items as well as aseembies/components that are installed into them

TCTOs are used to modify weapon systems, commodities, and equipment necessary to support the weapon system within specified time limits, initiate special one-time inspection to impose temporary restrictions and track support system and equipment configuration on systems or equipment
1 TCTO Types, Priorities, and Levels
One of three priorities is assigned to the TCTO; Immediate Action, Urgent Action, or Routine Action
4 LIFE CYCLE MANAGEMENT OF TCTOS

context_relevancy.query: Provide a table showing aircraft models with multi-tank fuel systems, detailing their internal fuel capacities and locations of fuel dump vents as specified in military refueling guidelines.
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 4 F-14 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURES
1 F-14 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURES
The SPR panel (Figure 4-1) includes a REFUEL/TRANSFER switch, two pre-check switches (FUS TANKS and WING/EXT TANKS) and a fuel vent pres- sure gauge
The aircraft has eight fuselage fuel tanks two wing box tanks, two integral wing tanks, and two external fuel tanks
1 Aircraft Fuel Dump (Jettison) Outlet
The exter- nal fuel tanks are vented through outlets on the tank bottoms
Refueling can be done with the wings swept forward or aft
CHAPTER 2 EA/A-6 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURES
1 EA/A-6 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURES
Figure 2-1 shows the refueling station switches that control fuel flow into the three fuselage tanks, five wing tanks, and up to four external tanks
Total internal fuel capacity is 2344 gallons; each external fuel tank holds an additional 300 gallons
Aircraft fuel dump outlets are located on the wing trailing edges near the wingtips and at the aft end of the fuselage
Fuel vent outlets are located in the aft fuselage and on the undersides of the wings near the wingtips
Three fuel tank over-pressurization lights are located on the fueling station door to warn of impending fuel tank over-pressurization The aircraft has no on board Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) or self-start capability, but one engine can start the other
CHAPTER 5 EF/F-18 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURES
1 EF/F-18 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURES
F-18C/D aircraft also include a REFUEL TEST switch (which will not be used during hot refueling) and a VENT TANK WET light with a test switch
1 Fuel Dump Outlets
The fuel dump outlets are on the trailing edges of the vertical stabilizers, while the external tanks have their own vents at the tank bottoms
Total internal fuel capacity (in four fuselage tanks and two wet wing tanks) is 1589 gallons
CHAPTER 3 E-2 HOT REFUELING
Fuel is carried in two wing tanks with a total capacity of 824 gallons
CHAPTER 6 S-3 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURE
1 S-3 HOT REFUELING PROCEDURE
The aircraft can carry 1933 gallons in four internal fuel tanks in the center wing section (inboard of the wing folds) and 265 gallons in each of two external fuel tanks

context_relevancy.query: Describe the relationship between pressure management and equipment integrity during aircraft refueling operations
context_chunks:
CHAPTER 1 SUPPORT EQUIPMENT FOR HOT REFUELING OPERATIONS
Do not hot refuel aircraft with a hot brake condition
The maximum refueling pressure is 55 pound-force per square inch (PSI)
Verify with the aircrew that fuel has not been dumped (jettisoned) on the previous flight
If fuel had been dumped and the position of the dump valve is not positively known to be closed, then aircraft will not be hot refueled
Aircraft high level shutoff valves must be pre-checked at the begin- ning of the hot refueling operation
During hot refueling, check that air is flowing from the aircraft fuel vent outlet(s) (except EF/F-18)
Make sure refueling is not done within 50 feet of other aircraft with engine(s) operating
Make sure aircraft is chocked
Fuel pressure from servicing equipment should not exceed 55 PSI, during normal refueling
Make sure aircraft is receiving correct fuel (JP-4, JP-5 or JP-8)
Ob- serve servicing vehicle pressure
If the aircraft crew requests shutting down refuel operation to transfer fuel between aircraft tanks, the single point poppet valves will be closed to prevent backflow into the refueling unit sump tank
Failure to comply could result in damage to, or destruction of, equipment or loss of mission effectiveness
When servicing is complete, deactivate deadman (deenergize pumps on Type II system), close single point nozzle(s), relieve pressure if required, and disconnect servicing nozzle(s) from aircraft
Prior to pressurizing the system, the SCR or his designated representative shall check the strainer coupling quick discon- nect locking, and shall ensure the refueling nozzle is securely locked to the aircraft by attempting to remove the nozzle with the poppet valve in the open position
If the refueling nozzle can be removed from the aircraft with valve open, the refueling unit/hydrant hose cart operator will be immediately notified to remove the nozzle from service
Failure to comply could result in damage to, or destruction of, equipment or loss of mission effectiveness
(4) *The CCS will inform all refuel team members the aircraft is ready for refueling
Do not exceed 55 Pounds per Square Inch (PSI) (Airbus 50 PSI), refueling pressure, as indicated at refueling source
Fail- ure to comply could result in damage to, or destruction of, equipment or loss of mission effectiveness
Refueling will be accomplished in accordance with applicable refueling directives
Position the aircraft on the hot refueling pit
Prior to refueling, check that no air is flowing from the external fuel tank vents (on the bottom of the external tanks)
This verifies that engine bleed air is not pressurizing the tanks
Failure to comply could result in dam- age to, or destruction of, equipment or loss of mission effec- tiveness
Ground the refueling equipment and bond to the aircraft
